name: Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular through the Sierra de Andújar natural park
name_original: Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular por el parque natural de la Sierra de Andújar
region_intro: Family friendly hiking trails around National Park Sierra de Andújar traverse a diverse landscape characterized by Mediterranean forests of holm oaks, cork oaks, and stone pines, alongside riparian areas along the Yeguas and Jándula rivers. The terrain includes gently undulating hills and rocky crags, with elevations ranging from 400 to 1,290 meters. This varied topography offers a range of natural features, including reservoirs like Jándula and El Encinarejo, open grassy areas, and scrubland, providing diverse settings for family hikes. Best family friendly hiking trails around National Park Sierra de Andújar The most popular family friendly hiking route is Encinarejo Dam loop from Los Llanos, a 3.8 miles (6.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 37 minutes to complete. This easy route follows the scenic Encinarejo Reservoir. Another top favourite among local hikers is Old Path Viewpoint – Membrillejo Stream loop from Santuario de la Virgen de la Cabeza, a moderate 8.3 miles (13.3 km) path. This route offers varied Mediterranean vegetation and views from the Old Path Viewpoint. Local hikers also love the Santuario Virgen de la Cabeza - circular through the Sierra de Andújar natural park, a 11.1 miles (17.8 km) trail leading through the heart of the natural park, often completed in about 5 hours 9 minutes. Hiking around National Park Sierra de Andújar is defined by extensive Mediterranean forests, picturesque river valleys, and numerous viewpoints. The network offers options for different ability levels, including easy family hikes and more challenging routes. The routes in National Park Sierra de Andújar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 40 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore National Park Sierra de Andújar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many family-friendly hiking trails are available in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: There are 10 family-friendly hiking trails detailed in this guide for National Park Sierra de Andújar, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging routes suitable for active families. You'll find a good mix to explore the diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can we expect on family hikes in Sierra de Andújar?
A: The trails in National Park Sierra de Andújar offer a diverse topography, from gently undulating hills to rocky crags. You'll often find well-preserved Mediterranean forests with holm oaks and cork oaks, riparian forests along the Yeguas and Jándula rivers, and open grassy areas. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17265976" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Dam loop from Los Llanos&lt;/a&gt; provide relatively flat paths around water, while others might involve more varied ascents and descents.

Q: Are there any easy or short trails suitable for families with young children or toddlers?
A: Yes, for families with young children or those looking for an easier outing, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17265976" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Dam loop from Los Llanos&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. It's an easy 6.2 km route with minimal elevation gain, perfect for a relaxed family walk.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or natural features we can see along the family trails?
A: Many trails offer opportunities to see significant landmarks and natural features. You might encounter the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4484492" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Ginés Hermitage&lt;/a&gt;, which offers splendid views of the Jándula river valley, or the picturesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5341650" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Obejo Bridge over the Yeguas River&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2862464" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is also a beautiful spot to visit, often featured on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21846323" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Reservoir – Encinarejo Dam loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular routes that are good for families?
A: Absolutely! Circular routes are great for families as they bring you back to your starting point. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17265976" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encinarejo Dam loop from Los Llanos&lt;/a&gt; is a fantastic easy circular option. For a slightly longer, moderate challenge, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27626042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Path Viewpoint – Membrillejo Stream loop from Santuario de la Virgen de la Cabeza&lt;/a&gt;, which traverses varied Mediterranean vegetation.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking with family in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: The best time to visit National Park Sierra de Andújar for family hiking is typically during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, making for more comfortable hiking conditions, and the landscapes are particularly vibrant. Summer can be very hot, while winter might bring cooler temperatures and occasional rain.

Q: Can we spot any wildlife on the family-friendly trails?
A: National Park Sierra de Andújar is a vital sanctuary for wildlife. While spotting the elusive Iberian lynx requires luck and patience, you have a good chance of seeing red and fallow deer, wild boar, rabbits, and various bird species, including eagles and vultures. Keep an eye out near the rivers for otters. Early mornings or late afternoons are often the best times for wildlife observation.

Q: Are there viewpoints along the family trails that offer scenic vistas?
A: Yes, the park is known for its stunning panoramic vistas. Many trails lead to or pass by viewpoints. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27626042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Path Viewpoint – Membrillejo Stream loop&lt;/a&gt; offers great views. Other notable viewpoints in the area include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5061639" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valley Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1278398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Pastores Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, providing excellent photo opportunities.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads for family hikes?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Generally, popular starting points for trails, especially those near attractions like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1279995" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Cabeza&lt;/a&gt; or the Encinarejo Dam, will have designated parking areas. It's always a good idea to check specific route details for parking information before you head out.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Reviewers often praise the park's well-preserved Mediterranean landscapes, the tranquility of the trails, and the opportunities for wildlife spotting. Many also appreciate the variety of routes, from gentle riverside walks to more challenging ascents, making it suitable for different family preferences.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the family-friendly hiking trails in National Park Sierra de Andújar?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on trails in National Park Sierra de Andújar, but they must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Always check specific park regulations or individual trail rules, especially in sensitive areas or during certain seasons, to confirm any restrictions.
tips: The Basilica of Nuestra Señora de la Cabeza, located on a promontory from which you can see the Sierra de Andújar, is the second most visited Andalusian sanctuary after the hermitage of El Rocío.
It is a trail that is located within the Sierra de Andújar Natural Park, and is not open to the public
